figures.caption: Table 1. Archive-level summary of ROMAN on the long-sequence UCR benchmark. For each backbone and each nontrivial ROMAN configuration S ∈ {2, 3, 4}, the table reports wins, ties, and losses against the corresponding S = 1 baseline. The accuracydifference columns are reported across datasets in percentage points as median [Q1, Q3]. The final two columns report median per-dataset training and inference time ratios relative to baseline.

Fig. 3. Synthetic mechanism studies for (c) multiscale interaction and (d) full positional invariance. Left: representative examples. Right: mean test accuracy over ten realizations, comparing the baseline representation with ROMAN at S = 4.

Fig. 4. Baseline (S = 1) vs. ROMAN-transformed data (S ∈ {2, 3, 4}) results for the MiniRocket and FCN Classifier models. We present the per-dataset UCR accuracies and the scaling of computational times with respect to S.
Table 2. Archive-level summary of ROMAN on the long-sequence UEA benchmark.

Fig. 5. Critical-difference diagrams for the five-model hard-voting ensemble study on UCR and UEA. Each diagram compares the eight ensemble variants within the corresponding archive: the four baseline-only ensembles and their four mixed-scale ROMAN counterparts. Lower average rank is better.

Fig. 2. Synthetic mechanism studies for (a) coarse position awareness and (b) longrange correlation. Left: representative examples. Right: mean test accuracy over ten realizations, comparing the baseline representation with ROMAN at S = 4.

Fig. 1. Overview of the ROMAN transformation. Left: schematic multiscale construction. Each scale s is partitioned into windows of common length Lbase, and the window index w records coarse temporal position within that scale. Right: Example of the stacked pseudochannels generated by applying ROMAN (S=2) to a real time series.
